canonical_summary:
An NRC nuclear reactor license takes between 16 and 48 months of formal review, depending on the licensing pathway and how much pre-application work the developer has completed. Under 10 CFR Part 50, recent advanced reactor construction permits have run from 16 months (Kairos Hermes 2, docketed July 2023 to permit November 21, 2024) to roughly 25 months (Kairos Hermes 1, November 29, 2021 to December 14, 2023). The TerraPower Natrium permit at Kemmerer, Wyoming took about 22 months from docketing in May 2024 to issuance on March 4, 2026, and the Long Mott Xe-100 review is on an 18-month NRC schedule under Executive Order 14300.

Under 10 CFR Part 52, both AP1000 Combined License reviews ran about 46 to 48 months: Vogtle 3 and 4 (docketed March 28, 2008; COLs NPF-91 and NPF-92 issued February 10, 2012) and V.C. Summer 2 and 3 (docketed March 27, 2008; NPF-93 and NPF-94 issued March 30, 2012). Under 10 CFR Part 53, finalized March 26, 2026 and effective April 29, 2026, the NRC targets review timelines of roughly 18 months or less.

Pre-application engagement is the single largest variable. Kairos invested roughly three years of topical reports before docketing, and the formal Hermes review completed three months ahead of its accelerated 21-month schedule.
h1: How Long Does an NRC License Take? Part 50, Part 52, and Part 53 Timelines
meta_title: How Long Does an NRC License Take? 2026 Timelines
meta_description: NRC nuclear reactor licenses take 16 to 48 months of formal review. Compare Part 50, Part 52, and Part 53 durations with primary-source dockets and dates.

sections.heading: The Three NRC Licensing Pathways and Their Nominal Timelines
paragraphs: The Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) licenses commercial reactors under three regulations in Title 10 of the Code of Federal Regulations: Part 50 (two-step construction permit then operating license, in force since 1956), Part 52 (one-step Combined License referencing a certified design, in force since 1989), and Part 53 (risk-informed, technology-inclusive framework, final rule effective April 29, 2026). Each pathway has a different nominal review duration, and each has been tested by recent advanced reactor and large light-water reactor projects.

The headline numbers below capture only the formal, post-docketing NRC review. They do not include pre-application engagement (often two to four years), state and local siting, environmental review preparation by the applicant, or post-license construction and operational readiness testing. They also do not include any applicable Early Site Permit or Design Certification, both of which can run in parallel or precede the application.
bullets: Part 50 Construction Permit (CP): historically 24 to 36 months; recent advanced reactor CPs landing at 16 to 25 months
Part 50 Operating License (OL): separate downstream review after construction, typically 12 to 24 months
Part 52 Combined License (COL): approximately 46 to 48 months for the first-of-a-kind AP1000 reviews
Part 53 (effective April 29, 2026): NRC target of roughly 18 months or less, with the first applications expected in the second half of 2026

sections.heading: Part 50 Construction Permit Timing: Kairos, TerraPower, and Long Mott
paragraphs: Part 50 is the two-step process used for the legacy U.S. fleet and chosen by most current advanced reactor demonstration projects. The applicant first obtains a Construction Permit, then later applies for an Operating License once the plant is built. Four recent advanced reactor CP applications define the modern timing benchmark.

subsections.heading: Kairos Power Hermes 1 (Docket 050-07513, Oak Ridge, Tennessee)
paragraphs: Kairos opened formal pre-application engagement with the NRC in November 2018 and submitted more than 11 topical and technical reports over the next three years on its fluoride-salt-cooled, pebble-bed KP-FHR design. The Hermes 1 CP application was submitted in September 2021 and docketed November 29, 2021. The Commission voted to approve issuance on December 12, 2023, and Construction Permit CPNPF-1 issued December 14, 2023. Formal review duration: approximately 25 months from docketing to issuance, after roughly three years of pre-application work.

subsections.heading: Kairos Power Hermes 2 (Dockets 050-00611 and 050-00612)
paragraphs: Hermes 2 is a two-unit follow-on to demonstrate electricity generation. Kairos submitted the CP application in July 2023, the NRC accepted it for review in late summer 2023, the Final Safety Evaluation Report (FSER) was completed in August 2024 (roughly four months ahead of schedule), and the Commission approved issuance on November 21, 2024. Formal review duration: approximately 16 months from docketing, the fastest modern non-light-water CP review on record, achieved by reusing the Hermes 1 safety basis.

subsections.heading: TerraPower Natrium, Kemmerer Unit 1 (Wyoming)
paragraphs: TerraPower submitted the Kemmerer Unit 1 CP application on March 28, 2024 for a 345 MWe sodium-cooled fast reactor with molten-salt thermal storage. The NRC docketed the application in May 2024, the first commercial non-light-water power reactor application docketed in over 40 years. Staff completed the safety review in December 2025, reportedly 11 percent under budget, and the Commission voted to issue the construction permit on March 4, 2026. Formal review duration: approximately 22 months from docketing, against an original 27-month schedule.

subsections.heading: Long Mott Generating Station, Xe-100 (Seadrift, Texas)
paragraphs: Long Mott Energy LLC, a Dow subsidiary, filed a CP application on March 31, 2025 for four X-energy Xe-100 high-temperature gas-cooled reactor modules (each approximately 80 MWe / 200 MWth) to supply power and steam to Dow's Seadrift complex. The NRC docketed the application on May 15, 2025 (docket NRC-2025-0079) and on June 16, 2025 published an 18-month review schedule, explicitly halving its generic CP timeline to meet Executive Order 14300. The target FSER date is November 2026, putting docketing-to-issuance near 18 to 20 months if the Commission vote follows promptly.

bullets: Hermes 1: docketed November 29, 2021; permit issued December 14, 2023 (about 25 months)
Hermes 2: docketed mid-2023; permits issued November 21, 2024 (about 16 months)
Natrium Kemmerer Unit 1: docketed May 2024; permit issued March 4, 2026 (about 22 months)
Long Mott Xe-100: docketed May 15, 2025; targeting FSER November 2026 (18-month schedule)

sections.heading: Part 52 Combined License Timing: Vogtle and V.C. Summer
paragraphs: Part 52 created a one-step Combined License (COL) that resolves construction and operation issues in a single proceeding, typically by referencing a previously certified design and an Early Site Permit. The first two Part 52 reviews were the AP1000 projects at Vogtle (Georgia) and V.C. Summer (South Carolina). Both followed near-identical four-year schedules.

subsections.heading: Vogtle Units 3 and 4 (COLs NPF-91 and NPF-92)
paragraphs: Southern Nuclear Operating Company submitted the Vogtle COL application on March 28, 2008. The NRC issued NUREG-2124 (Final Safety Evaluation Report) and NUREG-1947 (Final Supplemental Environmental Impact Statement). The mandatory uncontested hearing ran September 27 to 28, 2011. The Commission voted to issue the COLs on February 9, 2012, and the staff issued NPF-91 (Unit 3) and NPF-92 (Unit 4), together with Limited Work Authorizations LWA-001 and LWA-002, on February 10, 2012. The Federal Register notice appeared at 77 FR 12332 on February 29, 2012. Total: approximately 46 to 47 months.

subsections.heading: V.C. Summer Units 2 and 3 (COLs NPF-93 and NPF-94)
paragraphs: South Carolina Electric and Gas Company, jointly with Santee Cooper, submitted the V.C. Summer COL application on March 27, 2008 (the general information portion is in ADAMS at ML081300504). The NRC issued Combined Licenses NPF-93 (Unit 2) and NPF-94 (Unit 3) on March 30, 2012. The Federal Register notice appeared at 77 FR 21515 on April 10, 2012. Total: essentially 48 months. The Summer project was abandoned in construction in 2017 and the COLs were formally terminated March 6, 2019, but the licensing review itself completed on the Part 52 schedule.

bullets: Vogtle 3 and 4: docketed March 28, 2008; COLs issued February 10, 2012 (about 46.5 months)
V.C. Summer 2 and 3: docketed March 27, 2008; COLs issued March 30, 2012 (about 48.1 months)
Both reviews matched the NRC's stated under-48-month target for a design-certified-referencing COLA
Neither figure includes the underlying AP1000 Design Certification or the Early Site Permits the COLAs referenced

sections.heading: Part 53 Timing: First Applications Expected in 2026 to 2028
paragraphs: On March 26, 2026 the NRC Commissioners voted to finalize 10 CFR Part 53, the Risk-Informed, Technology-Inclusive Regulatory Framework for Advanced Reactors. The final rule was published in the Federal Register on March 30, 2026 and becomes effective on April 29, 2026. Part 53 is the first new initial reactor licensing pathway since Part 52 in 1989 and the first major rewrite of the licensing standard since the Atomic Energy Commission promulgated Part 50 in 1956.

Part 53 is optional. Developers may still pursue Part 50 or Part 52. The framework is technology-inclusive (any fuel form, coolant, size, or commercial end use), performance-based, and graded according to safety risk. Security, operator-staffing, and emergency-planning requirements are correspondingly graded. The NRC has stated that Part 53 is intended to compress design review timelines to roughly 18 months or less and to cut application costs by half or more relative to legacy pathways. The rule arrives more than a year ahead of the end-of-2027 statutory deadline Congress set in Section 103 of the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (NEIMA).
bullets: Final rule voted March 26, 2026; published March 30, 2026; effective April 29, 2026
Second half of 2026: first construction permit or operating license applications expected under Part 53
2027: larger cohort of small modular reactor, microreactor, sodium-cooled, high-temperature gas-cooled, and molten-salt developers expected to file
2027 to 2028: bulk of advanced reactor filings, including Combined License applications under Part 53
Several Part 50 dockets (Natrium Kemmerer, Long Mott Xe-100) are continuing under their original pathway rather than migrating

sections.heading: How Pre-Application Engagement Compresses Formal Review Time
paragraphs: The single largest determinant of NRC review duration is how much technical work the applicant resolves before docketing. The Kairos Hermes case is the clearest data point. Kairos opened pre-application discussions in November 2018 and submitted more than 11 topical reports plus a series of technical reports on mechanistic source term methodology, fuel qualification, principal design criteria, and reactor coolant chemistry over the next three years. Each topical report was reviewed and either approved or under active review before docketing, so the formal CP review could incorporate them by reference rather than relitigate first principles.

When the NRC docketed the Hermes 1 CP, staff committed to a 21-month review schedule, itself an acceleration relative to historical practice for first-of-a-kind non-light-water reactors. Staff completed the safety evaluation in 18 months, roughly three months ahead of the accelerated baseline. NRC News Release 23-034 (July 31, 2023) cited the result as evidence that risk-informed approaches improve efficiency without compromising safety. The same pattern repeated for Hermes 2, where the reuse of the Hermes 1 safety basis enabled a 16-month review.
bullets: Topical reports resolve generic methodology questions off the critical path of any single application
Iterative white paper exchanges surface staff concerns when applicants can still revise design choices cheaply
Familiarity reduces Request for Additional Information (RAI) volume during the docketed review
NRC draft white paper ML20281A761 targets approximately six months of formal review savings for substantive pre-application engagement
Following the ADVANCE Act of 2024 the NRC is updating Interim Staff Guidance to standardize voluntary pre-application engagement

sections.heading: What Slips NRC Review Time
paragraphs: Even with strong pre-application work, several factors routinely extend a formal review. Most are knowable in advance, and each maps to a concrete mitigation.
bullets: First-of-a-kind technology with no prior NRC staff exposure (Hermes 1 versus Hermes 2 is the canonical paired example)
Late or incomplete topical reports, which push methodology disputes into the docketed review as RAIs
Site-specific environmental issues not resolved through a prior Early Site Permit
Contested hearings before the Atomic Safety and Licensing Board, which can add months to a Part 52 schedule
Design changes mid-review, which trigger formal application amendments and re-noticing
Resource contention at the agency when multiple novel applications land in the same quarter
Insufficient Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA) inputs, particularly material under Part 53's risk-informed framework

sections.heading: Accelerators: NEIMA Fees, ARCAP/TICAP, Executive Order 14300, and AI Tools
paragraphs: Several mechanisms are now actively compressing NRC review duration beyond pre-application engagement alone. They are statutory, regulatory, and operational, and they stack.

subsections.heading: NEIMA Fee Carve-Outs and the Reduced Hourly Rate
paragraphs: The Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (S. 512, signed January 14, 2019) restructured how the NRC recovers its budget. Section 102(b)(1)(B) defines excluded activities the agency does not have to recover through licensee fees, including advanced reactor regulatory infrastructure activities. Section 201 of the ADVANCE Act (signed July 2024) amended NEIMA to require the NRC to assess a Reduced Hourly Rate to advanced reactor applicants and pre-applicants for qualifying activities. The Reduced Hourly Rate for pre-applicants sunsets September 30, 2030. The FY 2026 NRC fee rule (March 12, 2026) excludes approximately USD 23.8 million in advanced reactor regulatory infrastructure activities and USD 96.8 million in fee-relief activities from the fee base.

subsections.heading: ARCAP and TICAP Guidance
paragraphs: The Advanced Reactor Content of Application Project (ARCAP) and Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project (TICAP) standardize what goes into an advanced reactor application. By clarifying the minimum content and format expected, they reduce the iteration cycles between applicants and staff during acceptance review and shorten the early portion of the docketed review.

subsections.heading: Executive Order 14300 and the 18-Month Target
paragraphs: Executive Order 14300 set an 18-month statutory expectation for advanced reactor construction permit reviews. The NRC's June 16, 2025 schedule for Long Mott explicitly halved the agency's generic CP schedule to meet that target, and the Natrium and Hermes 2 reviews both came in well under the 27-month and 24-month historical benchmarks.

subsections.heading: AI and Workflow Tools
paragraphs: The NRC and applicants are piloting AI-assisted document review, automated RAI tracking, and workflow tools to compress acceptance review and reduce the time spent reconciling applicant submittals with staff guidance. These tools sit alongside ARCAP/TICAP and pre-application engagement rather than replacing them.

faqs.question: How long does an NRC license take in total?
faqs.answer: Formal NRC review for a reactor license runs roughly 16 to 48 months, depending on the pathway. A Part 50 Construction Permit for an advanced reactor has recently landed between 16 months (Kairos Hermes 2, issued November 21, 2024) and 25 months (Kairos Hermes 1, issued December 14, 2023). A Part 52 Combined License for the AP1000 took approximately 46 to 48 months (Vogtle and V.C. Summer, both docketed in March 2008 and issued in 2012). Part 53, effective April 29, 2026, targets approximately 18 months or less. These figures cover only NRC review and exclude pre-application work and post-license construction.

faqs.question: How long does a Part 50 Construction Permit take?
faqs.answer: Recent Part 50 Construction Permits for advanced reactors have completed in 16 to 25 months from NRC docketing to permit issuance. Kairos Hermes 1 took about 25 months (docketed November 29, 2021; issued December 14, 2023). Kairos Hermes 2 took about 16 months (docketed mid-2023; issued November 21, 2024). TerraPower Natrium at Kemmerer Unit 1 took about 22 months (docketed May 2024; issued March 4, 2026). The Long Mott Xe-100 review is on an 18-month NRC schedule from May 15, 2025 docketing, targeting a final safety evaluation in November 2026 under Executive Order 14300.

faqs.question: How long does a Part 52 Combined License take?
faqs.answer: The first two Part 52 Combined License reviews took approximately 46 to 48 months. Vogtle Units 3 and 4 were docketed March 28, 2008 and the COLs (NPF-91 and NPF-92) were issued February 10, 2012, about 46.5 months. V.C. Summer Units 2 and 3 were docketed March 27, 2008 and the COLs (NPF-93 and NPF-94) were issued March 30, 2012, about 48.1 months. Both matched the NRC's stated under-48-month target for a Combined License that references a certified design. These durations cover only the COL review and exclude the underlying AP1000 Design Certification and Early Site Permits.

faqs.question: What is the NRC's Part 53 timeline?
faqs.answer: The NRC Commissioners voted to finalize 10 CFR Part 53 on March 26, 2026. The final rule was published in the Federal Register on March 30, 2026 and becomes effective April 29, 2026. The agency is targeting design review timelines of roughly 18 months or less under Part 53, with application costs reduced by half or more relative to legacy pathways. The first Part 53 construction permit or operating license applications are expected in the second half of 2026, a larger cohort in 2027, and the bulk of advanced reactor filings including Combined License applications in 2027 to 2028.

faqs.question: Does pre-application engagement really reduce NRC review time?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pre-application engagement is the single largest variable in formal review duration. Kairos Power opened pre-application discussions in November 2018 and submitted more than 11 topical reports on its KP-FHR design over three years before docketing Hermes 1. The formal review then completed in 18 months against an accelerated 21-month schedule. The NRC's draft white paper ML20281A761 targets approximately six months of formal review savings plus expedited acceptance for applicants who pursue substantive pre-application engagement. The mechanism is that topical reports resolve methodology questions off the critical path of any single application.

faqs.question: How does the NEIMA Reduced Hourly Rate affect licensing costs?
faqs.answer: The Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (signed January 14, 2019) carved certain advanced reactor activities out of the NRC fee base. Section 201 of the ADVANCE Act (July 2024) amended NEIMA to require the NRC to assess a Reduced Hourly Rate to advanced reactor applicants and pre-applicants for qualifying activities. The Reduced Hourly Rate for pre-applicants sunsets September 30, 2030. The FY 2026 fee rule (March 12, 2026) excludes approximately USD 23.8 million in advanced reactor regulatory infrastructure activities and USD 96.8 million in fee-relief activities from the fee base, lowering effective costs for first-mover Part 53 developers.

faqs.question: How long did the Vogtle Combined License review take?
faqs.answer: The Vogtle Units 3 and 4 Combined License review took approximately 46.5 months. Southern Nuclear Operating Company submitted the COL application on March 28, 2008. The NRC published NUREG-2124 (Final Safety Evaluation Report) and NUREG-1947 (Final Supplemental Environmental Impact Statement). The mandatory uncontested hearing ran September 27 to 28, 2011. The Commission voted to issue the COLs on February 9, 2012, and the staff issued NPF-91 (Unit 3), NPF-92 (Unit 4), and Limited Work Authorizations LWA-001 and LWA-002 on February 10, 2012. The Federal Register notice appeared at 77 FR 12332 on February 29, 2012.

faqs.question: How long did the TerraPower Natrium permit at Kemmerer take?
faqs.answer: The Natrium Kemmerer Unit 1 Construction Permit review took approximately 22 months from docketing. TerraPower submitted the application on March 28, 2024. The NRC docketed it in May 2024, the first commercial non-light-water power reactor application docketed in over 40 years. Staff completed the safety review in December 2025, reportedly 11 percent under budget and ahead of the original 27-month schedule. The Commission voted to issue the construction permit on March 4, 2026 for the 345 MWe sodium-cooled fast reactor with molten-salt thermal storage. Submission to issuance was approximately 24 months.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Part 50 and Part 52?
faqs.answer: Part 50 is a two-step process from 1956: the applicant first obtains a Construction Permit, builds the plant, then applies separately for an Operating License. Part 52 (1989) consolidates both into a single Combined License (COL) that authorizes construction and operation upfront, typically by referencing a previously certified design and an Early Site Permit. Part 50 currently dominates advanced reactor demonstrations (Kairos, Natrium, Long Mott). Part 52 was used for the AP1000 builds at Vogtle and V.C. Summer. Part 53, effective April 29, 2026, adds a third option that is risk-informed and technology-inclusive.

faqs.question: Why did the Kairos Hermes 2 review go faster than Hermes 1?
faqs.answer: Hermes 2 (dockets 050-00611 and 050-00612) completed formal review in approximately 16 months, compared to 25 months for Hermes 1, because the NRC staff and Kairos reused much of the Hermes 1 safety basis. The KP-FHR technology, mechanistic source term methodology, fuel qualification, and principal design criteria had already been reviewed during Hermes 1, so first-encounter learning curve was absorbed before the Hermes 2 clock started. The NRC also piloted a simplified mandatory hearing process. The Final Safety Evaluation Report was completed in August 2024, roughly four months ahead of schedule, and the Commission approved issuance on November 21, 2024.

faqs.question: What slips NRC review time in practice?
faqs.answer: The biggest slips come from first-of-a-kind technology with no prior NRC staff exposure, late or incomplete topical reports that push methodology disputes into the docketed review as Requests for Additional Information (RAIs), site-specific environmental issues not resolved through a prior Early Site Permit, contested hearings before the Atomic Safety and Licensing Board, mid-review design changes that trigger formal application amendments, resource contention when multiple novel applications land in the same quarter, and insufficient Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA) inputs, which become particularly material under Part 53's risk-informed framework. Most are knowable in advance and map to concrete mitigations.

faqs.question: What does Executive Order 14300 require for NRC reviews?
faqs.answer: Executive Order 14300 set an 18-month statutory expectation for advanced reactor construction permit reviews. The NRC has begun implementing this directly in published schedules. On June 16, 2025 the NRC published an 18-month review schedule for the Long Mott Generating Station Xe-100 application (docketed May 15, 2025), explicitly halving the agency's generic CP schedule and targeting the final safety evaluation by November 2026. The Natrium Kemmerer Unit 1 review came in at roughly 18 months from docketing despite a 27-month plan, and Kairos Hermes 2 finished in 16 months. The 18-month target is now the operative benchmark for Part 50 CP reviews.
